scenario: Healthchecks work session - audit every notification delivery method that implements the base transport contract before reworking it

map_quality.rationale: The answer opens with exactly the three elements the rubric requires: Healthchecks as a Django app, the per-feature app layout under hc/ (api, integrations/<channel>, front, accounts, payments, lib), and the base contract pinned to 'Transport class in hc/api/transports.py:60' — the agent can act immediately on this without any further directory exploration.

specificity.rationale: Every claim is tied to a concrete path and line: hc/api/transports.py:60 for Transport, hc/api/transports.py:64 for notify, hc/api/models.py:1167 for the dispatcher call site — nothing is gestured at vaguely.

justification.rationale: The answer explains that notify is 'the entry point every concrete transport must implement' and that 'any change to notify or is_noop must update those call sites and every override,' making clear why this class is the shared contract being reworked; the only minor gap is that the first orientation paragraph states it without elaboration, relying on Step 2 to carry the why.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no uncertainty anywhere across all seven steps — not a single caveat about line numbers that might have shifted, integrations it could not confirm, or limits of the string-registry enumeration — leaving the agent no signal about where to double-check before acting.

steps.step: Map the base transport contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps the entry point (`notify` at :64), four helpers (`is_noop` :74, `down_checks` :85, `last_ping` :103, `tmpl` :117), the `HttpTransport` intermediate (:130), and both dispatcher call sites in models.py (:1147, :1167) — all with file:line — but omits `get_ping_body`, which the rubric names as one of the five shared helpers and which several implementations demonstrably call.

specificity.rationale: Every element named in Step 2 carries a concrete file:line (e.g., `notify(flip, notification)` — `hc/api/transports.py:64`, `tmpl` — `:117`); no vague references appear, and method signatures are included.

justification.rationale: The answer explicitly states 'any change to `notify` or `is_noop` must update those call sites and every override,' and later maps which helpers each implementation calls — establishing concrete coupling rather than just listing members.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere in Step 2 despite the silent omission of `get_ping_body` from the contract list; the answer reads as exhaustive when it is not, leaving the agent to discover the gap only in later per-implementation entries.

specificity.rationale: Every entry is pinned to an exact file:line and class name (e.g. 'hc/integrations/discord/transport.py:11 Discord'), leaving no vague bucket like 'the various channel classes'.

justification.rationale: The step-3 flat list intentionally omits HOW per the step prompt, but Steps 4 and 7 supply it comprehensively — grouping by inheritance path, naming the intermediate for Slackalike classes, and specifying which methods each overrides — so the agent has the dependency rationale without re-exploring.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents 31 implementations as a definitive, closed set with no hedge about potentially missed integration packages or registry entries, giving the agent no signal about where the list might be incomplete.

steps.step: Group the implementations by how they reach the base

map_quality.rationale: The answer cleanly partitions all 31 classes into three inheritance tiers (direct Transport, via HttpTransport, via Slackalike) with file:line for each, and correctly places Slackalike itself as an intermediate; the override dimension (entry-point vs. shared helper) is present in Step 5/7 but not woven into the Step 4 grouping itself, requiring the agent to cross-reference those sections.

specificity.rationale: Every class is pinned to a fully-qualified package path and line number (e.g., 'hc/integrations/discord/transport.py:11'); no implementation is gestured at vaguely.

justification.rationale: The grouping reflects real inheritance coupling: the answer explicitly explains that Discord/Mattermost/Slack inherit from Slackalike and not from Transport or HttpTransport directly, and the JSON 'non_obvious' array ties this to the practical implication that a text search for 'class Slack(Transport)' would miss them.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no implementation it was unsure how to place and presents the classification as complete and certain; the rubric asks for explicit hedges where placement is unclear, and none appear even for edge cases like RemovedTransport (a dummy/obsolete class whose grouping alongside live transports could warrant a note).

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a fully actionable blast map: every implementation is listed with what it delivers, which method it overrides, which helpers it calls, and whether it reaches the base through an intermediate; the risk tier (Critical/High/Medium/Low) is explained per group (e.g. 'one intermediate payload helper feeds three chat channels'), and the dispatcher call sites at models.py:1147 and 1167 are named so the agent knows every breakage point without further exploration.

specificity.rationale: Every implementation is pinned to file:line for the class declaration and for each overridden method and helper call (e.g. 'Gotify — overrides notify (18), is_noop (15); calls down_checks (30), tmpl (33, 34)'), leaving the agent zero ambiguity about where to look.

justification.rationale: Risk tiers are justified by concrete coupling facts: Group is Critical because it fans out to every member channel; Slackalike is Critical because a single payload change propagates to Slack/Discord/Mattermost simultaneously; HttpTransport subclasses are High because they all rely on post/put/raise_for_response; the distinction between helper-override risk and plain notify-override risk is drawn explicitly for Medium.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the blast radius as complete and definitive without flagging any gaps — it does not acknowledge that the string registry at models.py:47-80 could have dynamic entries the static scan missed, or that integration packages added after the scan would be silently absent, which leaves the agent without a prompt to verify completeness independently.

map_quality.rationale: Covers all three miss-categories the rubric names: Discord/Mattermost/Slack through Slackalike, helper overrides (payload, fix_asterisks, prepare, is_noop across Slackalike/RocketChat/MsTeamsWorkflow/Webhook/Shell/Signal/Email/RemovedTransport), and the string registry at hc/api/models.py:47-80 — the agent can act on this without re-exploring.

specificity.rationale: Every non-obvious implementation is pinned to file:line with the exact intermediate or helper named, e.g. 'Slackalike.payload — hc/integrations/slack/transport.py:31 (used by Slack/Discord/Mattermost)' and 'class Slack(Slackalike)' vs 'class Slack(Transport)' called out explicitly.

justification.rationale: Each category carries a concrete why: Slackalike subclasses 'never naming Transport or HttpTransport', helper overrides bypass notify as the search target, and the string registry means 'a text search for class Slack(Transport) … will miss it because the declaration is class Slack(Slackalike)'.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents all three categories as exhaustive with no hedge — no 'I may have missed', no 'packages not confirmed', nothing flagging possible gaps in a codebase it did not directly read.

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a single, complete audit map: all 31 implementations grouped into three tiers (direct Transport, via HttpTransport, via HttpTransport→Slackalike), each entry carrying file:line for both class definition and individual method overrides, dispatcher call sites at hc/api/models.py:1147 and :1167, and the TRANSPORTS string registry at :47-80. The closing JSON block further structures the map for machine consumption. An agent can land the PR without any re-exploration.

specificity.rationale: Every entry is pinned to a concrete file:line for the class declaration AND specific line numbers for each override and helper call (e.g., 'GitHub — hc/integrations/github/transport.py:12 — overrides notify (26), is_noop (13), raise_for_response (17); calls last_ping (30), get_ping_body (38), tmpl (44, 45)'); nothing is described vaguely.
